sop.coo_evaluation:
**You're at saturation on core ops—pivot to *leverage* what exists:**

• **Stop building SOPs; start a SOP *Audit & Refresh* cycle.** 56 SOPs need quarterly review for drift. Assign each coach ownership of 3-4 SOPs they use daily (sign-off on accuracy/relevance). This keeps docs alive and catches what's actually broken vs. theoretically complete.

• **Fill the 2 gaps fast but lean:** Email Newsletter + Blog Post are marketing *execution* tasks, not operational processes. Bundle them into one SOP: "Monthly Content Calendar & Publishing" (1 doc, 2-3 pages). Don't overthink—template + calendar view + approval step. Done in a week.

• **Build *outcome dashboards*, not more SOPs.** What you're missing isn't process—it's visibility. Create a monthly ops scorecard: client retention rate, coach utilization, facility maintenance compliance, marketing content velocity. This tells you where processes are *failing* faster than another SOP will.

• **Restructure your SOP *index* for coaches' actual workflow.** Your 7 time-based sections (Daily/Weekly/etc.) are admin logic, not user logic. Reindex by *role moment*: "Onboarding a new client," "Running a class," "Handling a complaint," "Month-end finance." Coaches navigate by what they're doing, not admin categories.

• **Create a *Coaches' Feedback Loop* SOP** (the missing operational piece). How do coaches flag broken processes, suggest changes, or escalate issues? Without this, your 17 coach SOPs ossify. Monthly 15-min pulse on what's not working.

**Next 30 days:** Newsletter SOP (quick win) + audit cycle kickoff + build the dashboard. That unlocks real operational insight.

kpi.advice:
# INCREASE REVENUE

• **Raise membership price immediately.** Your ARPU is £120.48 on 114 recurring members—that's weak for a specialist women's gym. Benchmark is £150–180/month. Test a £15/month increase on new joiners this month; existing members on 30-day notice. Should add £1,710+ monthly at full roll.

• **Convert trials to members aggressively.** You have 11 trials but only 114 recurring—your conversion rate is invisible from this data. Install a structured 7-day follow-up (text + call + class invite). Target 60% conversion; at current pricing that's +£800/month.

• **Launch a secondary revenue line.** Non-recurring revenue is missing. Introduce: PT packages (£40–60/session, 4-pack upfront), nutrition coaching (£30/month addon), or branded merchandise. Realistic target: £1,500–2,000/month new revenue.

• **Fill off-peak classes with incentive pricing.** Offer 6am and 2pm class passes at £8 (vs standard £12) to members who pre-book 4+ slots/week. Increases utilization and stickiness; minimal cost.

---

# REDUCE EXPENSES

• **People costs are 20.8% of revenue—cut to 18% max.** At £3,686/month on £17.7k revenue, you're over-staffed or paying above market. Audit: are you running unnecessary shifts? Can one trainer cover 2 consecutive classes? Aim for £3,200/month = save £486.

• **Non-people expenses at £9,390 (53% of revenue) are the real leak.** This is rent, utilities, insurance, equipment. Renegotiate rent (highlight zero churn as stability proof), shop energy suppliers (gym = high usage), and audit subscriptions/software—likely £200–400 waste.

---

# IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

• **Your profit margin is 5.28%—target 15–18% minimum.** You're running at break-even. Implement the revenue actions above (+£3,500

class_stats.suggestion:
1. **Move underperforming classes to peak days.** Shift Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle from Monday/Thursday to Wednesday/Tuesday slots where attendance is 50% higher. Test if these classes have timing or instructor issues, or if members simply prefer strength-focused offerings.

2. **Double down on Build classes during peak hours.** Your Build: Full Body, Upper, and Lower classes are consistently strong. Add an extra Build session on Wednesday or Tuesday evenings to capture demand you're currently missing.

3. **Investigate and potentially retire very low-attendance classes.** Barbelle (12), Strongevity (13), and Games Night (14) in the last 30 days suggest either poor promotion, wrong timing, or misaligned member interest. Either reposition them with a different instructor/time or reallocate those slots to proven formats.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Execute This Way

• **Start with your revenue reality, not your ego.** List every service you currently deliver (group classes, PT, nutrition coaching, challenges, etc.). Next to each, write actual numbers: how many members use it monthly, what you charge, what margin it generates. Kill anything that's been sitting unused for 3+ months. Grace, your menu should reflect what *actually sells*, not what you think should sell.

• **Build TWO separate menus with different language.** Coaches need the operational truth (class capacity limits, prep time, upsell triggers, commission structure). Members see the benefit story (what they *feel* and *achieve*, not features). Example: coaches see "PT session: 60 min, £50 cost, 40% margin"; members see "Transform Your Strength: 1-to-1 coaching tailored to your goals."

• **Price it now, even if imperfectly.** Don't workshop this endlessly. Take your top 3 services, set prices today based on: what you charge now + 10%, what competitors charge, what margin you need. Print a rough version this week. You'll adjust in 3 months when you have real uptake data—but waiting for "perfect pricing" costs you members and kills momentum.

• **Make it a sales tool, not a brochure.** Format it so a coach can hand it to a prospect in 20 seconds and close. Include a "bestseller" callout (your most popular service), a pricing anchor (your premium option makes mid-tier feel reasonable), and ONE clear CTA per section.

What's your current biggest revenue driver—group classes or PT?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Execution Plan for Grace

**1. Audit your actual drop-off points first**
Pull your analytics right now: which pages have the highest bounce rate? Which traffic sources underperform? (E.g., Instagram ads → landing page might have a mismatch.) Fix the bleeding before redesigning everything. Start with your *worst performer* only.

**2. Mine your own customer conversations for language**
Don't guess what "common questions" are. Spend 2 hours this week pulling:
- Emails/DMs where prospects asked before converting
- Questions from your free trial sign-ups
- What your staff hears repeatedly on tour calls
Use their exact words/phrasing in your page copy. This is worth 10x more than guessing.

**3. Add a single "what you get" visual hierarchy**
Women visiting need to see in 5 seconds: *class types* → *who it's for* (beginners? experienced?) → *proof it works* (testimonials from women like them). Right now, this is likely buried. Create a one-page cheat sheet showing exactly what a new member experiences Week 1, Week 4, Week 12.

**4. Create a simple FAQ section targeting your hesitations**
Not "common questions" — *objections*. Focus on: "Is this for complete beginners?" "What if I'm intimidated?" "Can I modify workouts?" "What's your cancellation policy?" Answer these *before* someone emails you.

**5. A/B test one element this week**
Change your CTA button text or the main headline on your worst-performing page. Measure for 2 weeks. Ship the winner. (Don

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System — Execution Blueprint for The Forge

• **Start with 3 questions, post-class QR code** — Don't build a "form." After classes, have instructors verbally ask one question (e.g., "What would make you come back more often?"), members scan a QR code, answer in 10 seconds. Instagram typeform or Google Form. Track which class times/instructors get mentioned most. This beats monthly forms because timing = honesty.

• **Tie feedback directly to retention money** — Tell Grace: every 5th piece of feedback mentioning "childcare" or "timing" becomes a concrete scheduling experiment. This isn't data collection—it's a profit signal. Implement the top complaint within 2 weeks, announce it to members as "you asked, we listened," and measure attendance lift.

• **Make instructors the filter, not admin** — Have instructors flag patterns (e.g., "3 people this week said 6pm slot is packed") in a 2-minute weekly debrief instead of Grace sifting through forms. Instructors become your early-warning system and feel ownership over member satisfaction. They'll naturally pitch solutions.

• **Post one insight publicly monthly** — Share one win on socials: "Members asked for 6am classes—we're launching them in January." Builds loyalty (members see action) and creates social proof for marketing new joins.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOPs for The Forge — Execution Blueprint

**1. Start with your HIGHEST-revenue service first (likely Personal Training)**
Don't try to build all five at once. Write the PT SOP this week, implement it, then move to the next. This prevents half-finished docs and lets you catch gaps faster. Use your top PT trainer to draft it—they know the real workflow, not theory.

**2. Structure each SOP around the MONEY MOMENT, not admin busywork**
For each service, identify: How does someone START? What's the sequence before payment clears? What's the 30-day retention checkpoint? Example: PT SOP should cover: intake → first session logistics → payment triggers → trainer assignment process. Skip the generic "be friendly" stuff.

**3. Build SOPs by SHADOWING, not guessing**
Spend 2 hours following your staff through a real membership signup, PT onboarding, and Fuel Forward order. Write down exactly what happens, what questions members ask, where delays happen. Your team knows what the SOP *should* be—you're just documenting it.

**4. Make one shared master doc (Google Drive/Notion) with checklists, not essays**
Each SOP = maximum 1-2 pages with checkboxes. Staff won't read a novel. Format: "Pre-Service" checklist → "During" checklist → "Post-Service" checklist → Contact/escalation rules. Fuel Forward SOP might be 3 sections: order placement, prep, delivery/pickup.

**5. Lock in accountability immediately after each draft**
Assign one staff member as "SOP owner" for each service. They own updates when things change

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with 8 weeks pre-planned, not 12** — Map out content directly tied to your membership cycle: new year joiners (Jan), summer body season (May-June), back-to-school energy (Sept). This gives you real angles to anchor posts around, not forced daily content. Use a simple Google Sheet with columns: Date | Platform | Content Type | Hook | CTA.

• **Batch-create on one day weekly** — Dedicate every Monday morning (90 mins) to filming 8-12 short videos of members, class clips, transformations, or you doing quick tips. Edit 4 of them that week, bank the rest. This kills the "what do I post today?" paralysis that kills consistency.

• **Own one content pillar that drives memberships** — Don't spread thin across "wellness tips, gym aesthetic, member spotlights, motivational quotes." Pick ONE: member transformations or real-life strength stories from YOUR members. Post 2-3x weekly on that, fill gaps with light content. This creates a recognizable brand people follow specifically for.

• **Use Instagram Reels + Facebook carousel ads as your 80/20** — Reels drive reach (post Tue/Thu 7pm), then boost your top 2 reels each month as ads targeting women 25-45 within 5 miles of Belfast for £5-10/day. Carousel ads convert better than single images for gym signups.

• **Link every post to a micro-conversion** — Not just "follow us." Every post needs: link to free trial booking, WhatsApp inquiry button, or email signup for a class schedule. Track which

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW — not later**
• Book the next 3 sessions back-to-back (8, 16, 24 weeks out). Get calendar invites to Jordan immediately. This kills the "I'll schedule it when I remember" spiral that kills momentum.

**Define the output, not the activity**
• Before Jordan arrives, clarify: 3-5 short-form clips (30-60 sec) for Instagram/TikTok, 1 longer piece (2-3 min) for website/email, or member testimonial footage? Vague briefs = wasted sessions. Give Jordan a shot list the day before.

**Tie it to your revenue cycle**
• Schedule sessions 2 weeks before your biggest membership push (January, summer, September). Footage that sits unused is footage that didn't grow revenue. Shoot to fuel campaigns, not to stock a library.

**Make Jordan's life easy (so he stays reliable)**
• Same time, same location each session. If it's 10am Tuesdays at The Forge, that's the rhythm. Low friction = he shows up, you get content, repeat.

**Assign ONE person to project-manage**
• You or a team member owns the brief, the reminder 48 hours before, the equipment check-in. Not "someone will handle it." Specific owner = sessions actually happen.

**The real move:** Book session 1 with Jordan today—call/text—and send calendar invites for #2 and #3 by EOD.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries for The Forge

**1. Audit your current leak points**
Map exactly where trial enquiries drop off NOW (email signup → follow-up → booking → first session → retention). Grace likely loses people in the 24-48hr gap. Fix that first before building automation—automate the *right* process, not a broken one.

**2. Build a 3-touch sequence, not a flood**
- Touch 1: Instant auto-reply (within 1 hour) confirming receipt + link to book induction slot directly
- Touch 2: Day 3 SMS if they haven't booked: "Reserved your spot—[booking link]. Questions? Call us."
- Touch 3: Day 10 email if still no show: "Your trial expires in 4 days. Last chance [link]."
Stop there. More feels desperate.

**3. Segment by response behavior in your automation**
If someone books immediately → different sequence (prep email, what to bring). If they ghost after enquiry → different sequence (social proof + gentle urgency). Use conditional logic in Zapier/Make/HubSpot—don't send the same message to everyone.

**4. Connect automation to actual capacity**
Pull trial slots from your booking system automatically so the system never oversells. Nothing kills conversion like "sorry, that time's gone." Automations must sync with your real schedule in real-time.

**5. Measure the ONE metric that matters**
Track "enquiry → first completed session" rate before and after. Target: 60%+. If it's lower, the automation isn't the problem—your onboarding experience is.

What system

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Role Checklists + KPIs for The Forge

**For JoJo (assume: Operations/Management)**
- List 5-7 weekly non-negotiables (member check-ins, staff briefing, facility audit, billing review)
- Attach ONE metric she owns: *New member onboarding completion rate* (target: 95% within 48hrs)
- Format: Simple Google Sheet or Notion—not a novel. Tick-box style.

**For Eilis (assume: Sales/Classes)**
- Weekly actions: Class schedule confirmation, social media content posting (3x minimum), one member retention call, trial bookings follow-up
- Her KPI: *Conversion rate from trial to paid membership* (track weekly; target: 40%+)
- Make her responsible for *one* metric, not five. Clarity beats comprehensiveness.

**For Sarah (assume: Finance/Admin)**
- Non-negotiables: Payroll processing, member account reconciliation, invoicing overdue clients, stock/supplies ordering
- Her KPI: *Payment processing speed* (invoices sent within 2 days of month-end) or *retention of repeat revenue* (recurring payments on time)

**Execution move:**
- Draft it in 30 mins yourself (don't workshop it to death)
- Share with each person individually first—ask "Can you actually do this weekly?" Get buy-in, not pushback
- Review together Friday afternoons (10 mins max) to reset for next week
- Print or pin in team area if you have one

**The real win:** This only works if Grace checks it *with them* weekly, not just files it.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session:**
- Send the SSO + 90-day plan to your team 48 hours ahead with this prompt: "Read this. Jot down one question or concern." Kills surprises, speeds up the real conversation.

**Structure it in three tight blocks (90 mins max):**
1. **SSO clarity (15 mins):** You walk through your single biggest win metric for next quarter—revenue target, member retention rate, whatever drives everything else. Make it one number they can repeat back.
2. **Their lane assignment (40 mins):** Go person-by-person. Don't say "you own member experience"—say "You own reducing no-shows by running Tuesday/Thursday check-in calls and testing a new pre-class reminder system." They need to see their fingerprints on the 90-day wins.
3. **Dependencies + blockers (25 mins):** Ask straight: "What do you need from me or someone else to win your lane?" Uncover the friction *now*.

**The trap to avoid:**
- Don't let this become a cheerleading pep talk. That's forgettable. Focus on conflict resolution—if two lanes overlap (like sales vs. retention), hammer it out *in the room* so people leave with one clear answer, not confusion.

**Follow-up (critical):**
- Send a 1-pager recap within 24 hours: SSO, each person's lane in one sentence, key blockers and who owns solving them. Reference it in 1-to-1s.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit for The Forge — 30-Day Rebalance

**1. Map actual hours, not job titles (Do this first)**
- Have each team member (instructors, reception, management) log *actual* time spent on each activity for 5 working days using a simple spreadsheet. Guesswork fails here. You'll likely find staff doing tasks outside their role (e.g., instructors handling admin because no one else will).

**2. Identify the real bottleneck — it's usually one person**
- Look for anyone consistently working >40 hours/week or covering multiple roles. In smaller gyms, this is often the owner or senior instructor doing reception + class prep + member follow-ups. That's your first rebalance target.

**3. Rebalance by task shifting, not hiring**
- Don't add headcount yet. Instead: move reception admin to quieter staff hours, batch member check-ins into one person's role, or swap who handles class logistics. Document which tasks *must* happen vs. which are "nice to have" — cut the latter if staff is drowning.

**4. Create a visible rota showing who owns what**
- Post a simple matrix (Google Sheet) showing: class scheduling, member follow-ups, cleaning, billing, community outreach. Make it public so team sees the rebalance and knows who to ask for what.

**5. Measure within 2 weeks, not 30**
- Check in at day 14. Did hours drop for the overloaded person? Did anything break? Adjust before the full 30 days ends. Quick wins build team buy-in.

What's your gut feeling on who's most stretched right

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Do an immediate spend audit (today):**
- Pull last 3 months of bank statements, categorize every transaction. You'll spot 2-3 quick wins (gym supplies ordered at wrong price point, unused software subscriptions, energy waste). This takes 90 minutes and typically finds 3-5% savings immediately with zero operational impact.

**Target payroll efficiency without cutting hours:**
- Map when members actually use the gym (pull your booking data). Shift your staff schedule to match demand peaks precisely—don't staff equally across all hours. Renegotiate one part-time role as on-call/flexible instead of fixed. Typical gym sees 4-6% labor savings this way.

**Renegotiate vendor contracts (this month):**
- Call your top 3 suppliers (equipment, cleaning, utilities)—say you're reviewing options and need their best pricing. You've got leverage as an established local business. Aim for 8-12% discounts by bundling or extending terms. Even one vendor dropping 15% gets you most of the way there.

**Cut one underperforming service/product line:**
- Which class or offering has lowest uptake or margins? Classes with <5 regular attendees or retail items gathering dust cost you shelf space and admin time. Kill it. Redirect that overhead to what actually drives revenue.

**Quick finance check:**
- Is Grace paying herself a salary from The Forge when she shouldn't be yet? That's often the hidden 10% right there—redirect to reinvestment.

Which of these feels most doable in the next 2 weeks?

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Set a fixed audit day (e.g., 3rd of each month)** — Block 90 mins right after month-close. Pull your 3 core metrics FIRST (member revenue, churn rate, class utilization %) before touching anything else. This prevents analysis paralysis and keeps you focused on what actually moves The Forge.

• **Create a one-page comparison sheet, not a spreadsheet novel** — Month-over-month delta only (e.g., "Members: 127 → 134 (+5.5%)"). Highlight red flags in bold. Grace doesn't need 12 tabs; she needs 3 KPIs she can defend to a bank or investor in 60 seconds.

• **Link KPIs directly to your next action** — Don't just track churn; when it ticks up, auto-trigger a 15-min "why?" conversation with your class instructors that week. Make the numbers *force* a decision, not sit in a file.

• **Use a shared Slack/Teams message or simple Google Sheet** — Post the monthly summary where your team sees it (not hidden in email). Transparency builds accountability and surfaces issues your staff notice that spreadsheets miss.

• **Schedule a 20-min monthly debrief with yourself** — Review the prior month's KPIs *and* the action you took. Did member growth stick? Did cost-cutting stick? This closes the loop and stops you chasing vanity metrics.

**When's your first audit month?** Lock the date now.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull 12 months of actual membership data NOW** — revenue, churn rate, peak months — into a simple spreadsheet. Don't wait for perfect data. Use credit card processor statements + gym management software exports. This becomes your baseline forecast anchor.

• **Build a 3-scenario model (conservative/realistic/aggressive)** tied to YOUR growth lever: If you're targeting 20% member growth this year, model what that means for monthly revenue with realistic churn built in. Revenue forecasting is only useful when it's tied to actions you control (pricing changes, new cohorts, retention pushes).

• **Monthly cash runway forecast is more important than annual profit** — track when you'll need to pay for equipment, staff bonuses, or marketing spend. Women's fitness markets are seasonal (New Year spike, summer dip). Know your cash valley 6 months out so you're not caught scrambling.

• **Use forecasting as a decision filter, not a crystal ball** — when you're considering spending £2K on a marketing campaign or hiring a PT, ask: "Does this move the needle in my forecast enough to justify it?" Wrong mindset = throwing money at growth. Right mindset = every spend has a forecast outcome attached.

• **Review & adjust monthly, not annually** — compare actual vs. forecast by member count and revenue. When reality diverges, update assumptions immediately. This keeps you agile, not stuck to guesses.

What's your current membership base and revenue range? That shapes which forecast scenario matters most.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**The actual work:**

• **Start with revenue buckets TODAY** — Pull last month's income statement and split it three ways: (1) class packages/memberships, (2) PT sessions, (3) add-ons (retail, nutrition coaching, merchandise). Don't wait for perfect data — use your bank statements + Mindbody/whatever software you use. Rough is fine.

• **Cost allocation that matters** — Classes cost: studio rent (split by % capacity used), instructor pay, music/tech. PT costs: instructor pay + small % of rent. Add-ons: COGS + time. Don't overthink it. A PT session generating £50/hour revenue that costs you £20 in instructor time is obviously healthier than a class generating £200/week but using 40% of your prime-time floor space.

• **Margin ranking in a spreadsheet** — Create three columns: service, gross revenue (last month), direct costs, gross margin %. Rank by margin %. This shows you immediately which service funds the gym and which is just filling the schedule. PT almost always wins here.

• **The conversation starter** — Once you see the numbers, ask: "Which service would I rather have more of?" If PT is 60% margin but only 15% of revenue, that's your growth signal. If classes are 25% margin but feel essential for community, you now KNOW the trade-off you're making.

• **Action this week** — Grab a spreadsheet, spend 90 minutes pulling numbers, share results with one trusted advisor (accountant/mentor). That's your baseline. Everything else builds from here.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automation Flow

• **Run a test enquiry TODAY** — submit a dummy email/form yourself, then track the exact sequence of emails/messages that should hit (welcome → questionnaire → trial booking → confirmation). Document every step and timestamp. This shows you what *actually* happens vs. what you *think* happens.

• **Export your automation triggers side-by-side** — pull the full setup from your platform (Zapier, ActiveCampaign, whatever you're using). Look for overlapping triggers on the same action (e.g., two "new form submission" workflows both firing). Kill the duplicate, keep the one doing the most work. Screenshot before/after so you can revert if needed.

• **Identify the "drop-off cliff"** — check your email open rates and click-through rates between each stage. Where does engagement die (usually the questionnaire or booking link)? That's your real problem, not just duplicates. Fix *that* first before optimizing flow.

• **Set a 30-minute audit window, not a project** — don't overthink this. Give yourself half an hour to map it, find obvious dupes, test, and fix. Then move on. You can refine later; right now you need to know if the pipe is actually broken.

• **Assign ONE person as "automation owner"** — even if it's you initially. Someone needs to check this monthly and own the results (trial conversions). Ownership kills drift.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — 3 Tasks to Lock In

**1. Member Check-In & Class Booking Reminders**
- Deploy a WhatsApp bot (Twilio + ChatGPT API) that sends class reminders 24h before booked sessions and auto-confirms cancellations
- *The ROI:* No-shows drop ~20-30%. You reclaim 5-8 hours/week of admin texting
- *Action today:* Export your current member list and last 30 days of booking data — this is your bot's training dataset

**2. Inquiry Response Automation (Warm Lead Nurture)**
- Set up an AI email responder (Zapier + ChatGPT) that responds to "How much is membership?" and "Do you offer classes for beginners?" within 5 minutes, 24/7
- Include your trial offer + link to book. Routes qualified leads to Grace only if they ask custom questions
- *The ROI:* You capture warm leads sleeping in your inbox; conversion jumps because response is instant
- *Action today:* Grab your last 50 inquiries. Identify the 5-6 repetitive questions. These become your AI prompt templates.

**3. Social Media Caption + Hashtag Generation**
- Use Claude or ChatGPT to generate Instagram captions (with emojis + relevant hashtags for Belfast women's fitness) from class descriptions or member wins you feed it
- *The ROI:* You post 4x more consistently without burning out; engagement compounds
- *Action today:* Create a simple Airtable form where your team drops "member story" or "new class" info —

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security Execution for The Forge Website

• **Pick ONE vulnerability audit first** — Use Sucuri (free scan) or scan.sitelock.com to identify actual weak points (outdated plugins, SSL issues, weak passwords) before spending money. Takes 30 mins, gives you the real problem to fix.

• **Force password reset + 2FA immediately** — Any team member accessing your booking/member payment system needs a strong password + authenticator app (Google Authenticator, free). This alone stops 99% of intrusions. Do it this week.

• **Get SSL certificate enforced** (if not already) — Ensure your site forces HTTPS across all pages. Most hosts include this free; just activate it in settings. Customers see the padlock; payment processors require it.

• **Backup your website daily, automated** — Use your hosting's backup feature or UpdraftPlus plugin (£10/month) set to automatic. If you get hacked, you restore within hours instead of losing everything.

• **Outsource the heavy lifting, not the thinking** — Pay a WordPress security specialist (£200-500 one-time audit) to harden your site *properly*, then maintain it yourself with the habits above. Cheaper than a data breach killing your reputation with members.

**Start with the audit this week. Everything else follows.**
